title: NEURAL NETWORKS WITH TRAINABLE MATRIX ACTIVATION FUNCTIONS [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## NEURAL NETWORKS WITH TRAINABLE MATRIX ACTIVATION FUNCTIONS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

The training process of neural networks usually optimizes weights and bias parameters of linear transformations, while nonlinear activation functions are prespecified and fixed. This work develops a systematic approach to constructing matrix activation functions whose entries are generalized from ReLU. The activation is based on matrix-vector multiplications using only scalar multiplications and comparisons. The proposed activation functions depend on parameters that are trained along with the weights and bias vectors. Neural networks based on this approach are simple and efficient and are shown to be robust in numerical experiments.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

In recent decades, deep neural networks (DNNs) have achieved significant successes in many fields such as computer vision and natural language processing (Voulodimos et al., 2018), (Otter et al., 2018). The DNN surrogate model is constructed using recursive composition of linear transformations and nonlinear activation functions. To achieve good performance, it is essential to choose activation functions suitable for specific applications. In practice, Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) is one of the most popular activation functions for its simplicity and efficiency. A drawback of ReLU is the presence of vanishing gradient in the training process, known as dying ReLU problem (Lu et al., 2019). Several relatively new activation approaches are proposed to overcome this problem, e.g., the simple Leaky ReLU, and Piecewise Linear Unit (PLU) (Nicolae, 2018), Softplus (Glorot et al., 2011), Exponential Linear Unit (ELU) (Clevert et al., 2016), Scaled Exponential Linear Unit (SELU) (Klambauer et al., 2017), Gaussian Error Linear Unit (GELU) (Hendrycks & Gimpel, 2016).

Although the aforementioned activation functions are shown to be competitive in benchmark tests, they are still fixed nonlinear functions. In a DNN structure, it is often hard to determine a priori the optimal activation function for a specific application. In this paper, we shall generalize ReLU and introduce arbitrary trainable matrix activation functions. The effectiveness of the proposed approach is validated using function approximation examples and well-known benchmark datasets such as CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100. There are a few classical works on adaptively tuning of parameters in the training process, e.g., the parametric ReLU (He et al., 2015b). However, our adaptive matrix activation functions are shown to be competitive and more robust in those experiments.

## 1.1 PRELIMINARIES

We consider the general learning process based on a given training set _{_ ( _xn, fn_ ) _}[N] n_ =1[,][where][the] inputs _{xn}[N] n_ =1 _[⊂]_[R] _[d]_[and][outputs] _[{][f][n][}][N] n_ =1 _[⊂]_[R] _[J]_[are][implicitly][related][via][an][unknown][target] function _f_ : R _[d] →_ R _[J]_ with _fn_ = _f_ ( _xn_ ). The ReLU activation function is a piecewise linear function given by

_σ_ ( _t_ ) = max _{t,_ 0 _} ,_ for _t ∈_ R _._ (1)

In the literature _σ_ is acting component-wise on an input vector. ## 2 TRAINABLE MATRIX ACTIVATION FUNCTION

Having a closer look at ReLU _σ,_ we observe that the activation _σ ◦ ηℓ_ ( _x_ ) = _σ_ ( _ηℓ_ ( _x_ )) could be realized as a matrix-vector multiplication _σ ◦ ηℓ_ ( _x_ ) = _Dℓ_ ( _ηℓ_ ( _x_ )) _ηℓ_ ( _x_ ) or equivalently _σ ◦ ηℓ_ = ( _Dℓ ◦ ηℓ_ ) _ηℓ_ , where _ηℓ_ is _column_ vector-valued in R _[n][ℓ]_ and _Dℓ_ is a _diagonal_ matrix-valued function mapping from R _[n][ℓ]_ to R _[n][ℓ][×][n][ℓ]_ with entries from the discrete set _{_ 0 _,_ 1 _}_ . This is a simple but quite useful observation. There is no reason to restrict on _{_ 0 _,_ 1 _}_ and we thus look for a larger set of values over which the diagonal entries of _Dℓ_ are running or sampled. With slight abuse of notation, our new DNN approximation to _f_ is calculated using the following recurrence relation

Here each _Dℓ_ is diagonal and is of the form

where _αℓ_ is a nonlinear function to be determined. Since piecewise constant functions can approximate a continuous function within arbitrarily high accuracy, we choose _αℓ_ to be the following step function

where _m_ is a positive integer and _{tℓ,j}[m] j_ =0[and] _[{][s][j][}][m] j_ =1[are][constants.][For][the][time][being,][let] _Dℓ_ ( _ηℓ_ ( _x_ )) _ηℓ_ ( _x_ ) = _σℓ_ ( _ηℓ_ ( _x_ )) with _σℓ_ being a scalar-valued function applied to _ηℓ_ ( _x_ ) componentwise. In the following, we list several _σℓ_ s corresponding to special _αℓ_ and visualize them in Figure 1.

In our case, we will choose the grid points _{sj}[m] j_ =1[a priori and train the step values] _[ ∪][L] ℓ_ =1 _[{][t][ℓ,j][}][m] j_ =0 of _αℓ_ . In such a way, the resulting DNN may use different activation functions for different layers, and these activation functions are naturally adapted to the target function _f_ and the target dataset. Since ReLU and Leaky ReLU are included by our DNN as special cases, the proposed DNN is clearly not worse than the traditional ones in practice. In the following, we call the neural network in equation 4, with the activation approach given in equation 5 and equation 6, a DNN based on the “Trainable Matrix Activation Function (TMAF)”.

## REMARK

_The activation functions used in TMAF neural network are not piecewise constants. Instead, TMAF activation is realized using matrix-vector multiplication, where entries of those matrices are trainable piecewise constants. There exist several trainable activation functions in the literature, e.g., Parametric ReLU (He et al., 2015b), Pad´e Activation Unit (PAU) (Molina et al., 2020), Piecewise Linear Unit (PWLU) (Zhou et al., 2021), Swish (Ramachandran et al., 2017), adaptive rational functions (Boull´e et al., 2020), adaptive splines (Bohra et al., 2020), adaptive activation functions for physics informed neural networks (ada, 2020). We point out that TMAF is different from the aforementioned activation approaches. One might think TMAF is similar to PWLU because they are both defined piecewise. However, by construction TMAF includes discontinuous functions as special cases (see Figure 1) while PWLU always results in a_ continuous _piecewise linear function with trainable slopes._

Starting from the diagonal activation _Dℓ_ , we can go one step further to construct more general activation matrices. First we note that _Dℓ_ could be viewed as a nonlinear operator _Tℓ_ : [ _C_ (R _[d]_ )] _[n][ℓ] →_ [ _C_ (R _[d]_ )] _[n][ℓ]_ , where

There seems to be no special reason, aside from keeping the computational cost as low as possible, to consider only diagonal operators _D_ . A more ambitious approach is to consider a trainable nonlinear activation _operator_ determined by more general matrices, for example, by a tri-diagonal operator

for _x ∈_ R _[d] ._ The diagonal entry _αℓ_ is given in equation 6 while the off-diagonal entries _βℓ_ , _γℓ_ are piecewise constant functions defined in a fashion similar to _αℓ_ . Theoretically speaking, even trainable full matrix activation is possible despite of potentially huge training cost. In summary, a DNN based on trainable nonlinear activation operators _{Tℓ}[L] ℓ_ =1[reads]

As we indicated above, TMAF can be used with any matrix and this will lead to a great flexibility in training and approximation. In fact, if a sparse matrix _D_ is used, so that the matrix-vector multiplication is proportional to the number of rows in the matrix, then the resulting algorithm will be computationally efficient. We can choose p-diagonal matrices, as well as matrices with prescribed sparsity patterns. Clearly, based on the simple examples we have considered here, one may conclude that such possibilities, when investigated in depth, can increase the robustness and the accuracy of the TMAF-NN.

## REMARK

_Our observation also applies to an activation function σ other than ReLU. For example, we may rescale σ_ ( _x_ ) _to obtain σ_ ( _ωi,ℓx_ ) _using a set of constants {ωi,ℓ}_ 1 _≤i≤nℓ,_ 1 _≤ℓ≤L varying layer by layer and neuron by neuron. Then σ_ ( _ωi,ℓx_ ) _are used to form a matrix activation function and a TMAF DNN, where {ωi,ℓ} are trained according to given data and are adapted to the target function._

## 3 NUMERICAL RESULTS

In this section, we demonstrate the feasibility and efficiency of TMAF by comparing it with traditional ReLU-type activation functions. Recall that neurons in the _ℓ_ -th layer will be activated by the matrix _Dℓ_ . In principle, all parameters in equation 6 are allowed to be trained. To ensure practical efficiency, we shall fix interval grid points _{sj}[m] j_ =1[used in] _[ α][ℓ]_[and only let function values] _[ {][t][ℓ,j][}]_[ in] equation 6 be trained in the following. In each experiment, we use the same learning rates, stochastic optimization methods, and number NE of epochs (optimization iterations). In particular, the learning rate is 1e-4 from epoch 1 to[NE] 2[and 1e-5 is used from epoch][NE] 2[+ 1][ to][ NE][.][The optimization] method is ADAM (Kingma & Ba (2015)) based on mini-batches of size 500. Numerical experiments are performed in PyTorch (Paszke et al. (2019)). We provide two sets of numerical examples:

- Function approximations by TMAF networks and ReLU-type networks;

- Classification problems for MNIST and CIFAR set solved by TMAF and ReLU networks.

For the first class of examples we use the _ℓ_[2] -loss function as defined in equation 3. For the classification problems we consider the _cross-entropy_ that is widely used as a loss function in classification models. The cross entropy is defined using a training set having _p_ images, each with _N_ pixels. Thus, the training dataset is equivalent to the vector set _{zj}[p] j_ =1 _[⊂][R][N]_[with each] _[ z][j]_[being an image.][The] _j_ -th image belongs to a class _cj ∈{_ 1 _, . . . , M }_ . The neural network maps _zj_ to _xj ∈_ R _[M]_ ,

The cross entropy loss function of _ηL,W,B_ then is defined by

3.1 APPROXIMATION OF A SMOOTH FUNCTION

As our first example, we use neural networks to approximate

The training datasets consist of 20000 input-output data pairs where the input data are randomly sampled from the hypercube [ _−_ 2 _,_ 2] _[n]_ based on uniform distribution. The neural networks have single or double hidden layers. Each layer (except input and output layers) has 20 neurons. For TMAF _Dℓ_ in equation 5, the function _αℓ_ uses intervals ( _−∞, −_ 1 _._ 4), ( _−_ 1 _._ 4 _, −_ 0 _._ 92], ( _−_ 0 _._ 92 _, −_ 0 _._ 56], ( _−_ 0 _._ 56 _, −_ 0 _._ 26], ( _−_ 0 _._ 26 _,_ 0], (0 _,_ 0 _._ 26], (0 _._ 26 _,_ 0 _._ 56], (0 _._ 56 _,_ 0 _._ 92], (0 _._ 92 _,_ 1 _._ 4], (1 _._ 4 _, ∞_ ) such that probability over each of the ten intervals is 0.1 with respect to Gaussian distribution. Moreover, we apply BatchNorm1d in PyTorch to the linear output of neural networks in each hidden layer. The approximation results are shown in Table 1 and Figures 2–3, where Para-ReLU stands for the parametric ReLU neural network. It is observed that TMAF is the most accurate activation approach in these examples.

## 3.2 APPROXIMATION OF AN OSCILLATORY FUNCTION

_f_ ( _x_ ) = sin(100 _πx_ ) + cos(50 _πx_ ) + sin( _πx_ ) _,_ (9)

see Figure 4a for an illustration. In fact, the function in equation 9 is rather difficult to capture by traditional approximation methods as it is highly oscillatory. We consider ReLU, parametric ReLU,

For this challenging problem, we note that the diagonal TMAF and tri-diagonal TMAF produce high-quality approximations (see Figures 5 and 4b) while ReLU and parametric ReLU are not able to approximate the highly oscillating function within reasonable accuracy, see Figure 4b and Table 2. Moreover, it is observed from Figure 6 that ReLU and parametric ReLU actually approximate the low frequency part of the target function. To capture the high frequency, ReLU-type neural networks are clearly required to use much more neurons, introducing significantly amount of weight and bias parameters. On the other hand, increasing the number of intervals in TMAF only lead to a few more training parameters.

## 3.3 CLASSIFICATION OF MNIST AND CIFAR DATASETS

We now test TMAF by classifying images in the MNIST, CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 dataset.
For the MNIST set, we implement double layer fully connected networks defined as in equation 2 and equation 4 with 10 neurons per layer (except at the first layer _n_ 0 = 764), and we use ReLU or

6

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

**----- Start of picture text -----**

(a) ReLU approximation (b) Para ReLU approximation
**----- End of picture text -----**

Figure 6: Approximations to sin(100 _πx_ ) + cos(50 _πx_ ) + sin( _πx_ ), ReLU-type

diagonal TMAF as described in Subsection 3.1. Numerical results are shown in Figures 7a, 7b and Table 3. We observe that performance of the TMAF and the ReLU networks are similar. Such a behavior clearly should be expected for a simple dataset such as MNIST.

For the CIFAR-10 dataset, we use the ResNet18 network structure provided by (He et al., 2015a). The activation functions are still ReLU and the diagonal TMAF used in Subsection 3.1. Numerical results are presented in Figures 8a, 8b and Table 3. Those parameters given in (Paszke et al., 2019) are already tuned well with respect to ReLU. Nevertheless, TMAF still produces smaller errors in the training process and returns better classification results in the evaluation stage, see Table 3.

For the CIFAR-100 dataset, we use the ResNet34 network structure provided by (He et al., 2015a) with the ReLU and TMAF activation functions in Subsection 3.1. Numerical results are presented in Figures 9a and 9b. In the training process, TMAF again outperforms the classical ReLU network.

It is possible to improve the performance of TMAF applied to those benchmark datasets. The key point is to select suitable intervals in _αℓ_ to optimize the performance. A simple strategy is to let those intervals in equation 6 be varying and adjusted in the training (or the evaluation) process, which will be investigated in our future research.

zero_shot__claude-opus-4.7.label: Zero Shot (claude-opus-4.7)
zero_shot__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback: This paper proposes a 'Trainable Matrix Activation Function' (TMAF) that generalizes ReLU by replacing the implicit diagonal {0,1}-matrix with a diagonal (or more general) matrix whose entries are trainable piecewise-constant functions. The idea is simple and intuitive, and the numerical experiments suggest some advantage, particularly on oscillatory function approximation. However, the paper suffers from several issues: (1) key equations referenced in the text (equation 4, equation 5, equation 6, equation 7) are not visibly rendered in the submitted text, making it hard to verify the precise formulation; (2) the construction of the piecewise-constant function α_ℓ is underspecified—the relationship between the m+1 values {t_{ℓ,j}} and the m grid points {s_j} and the resulting number of pieces is ambiguous; (3) a clear typo/inconsistency in the MNIST experiment (n_0 = 764 instead of 784); (4) the claim that TMAF is 'not worse than' traditional networks because it 'includes' ReLU as a special case overstates what has been demonstrated—this holds only for the function class, not for trained networks; (5) the parametric ReLU comparison on the oscillatory function achieving exactly 1.00 loss seems suspicious and is not discussed; and (6) the tri-diagonal TMAF interval specification contains notation that appears garbled. The ideas are reasonable but presentation and specification need substantial tightening.

comments.title: MNIST input dimension inconsistency (764 vs 784)
comments.quote: we implement double layer fully connected networks defined as in equation 2 and equation 4 with 10 neurons per layer (except at the first layer _n_ 0 = 764), and we use ReLU or
comments.explanation: MNIST images are 28×28 = 784 pixels, so n_0 should be 784. The value 764 appears to be a typo. Since this is a basic, well-known dataset parameter, it is almost certainly an error rather than an unusual preprocessing choice. It is localized, but it shows a lack of care that makes the reader question other numbers.

comments.title: Definition of piecewise-constant α_ℓ is underspecified
comments.quote: where _m_ is a positive integer and _{tℓ,j}[m] j_ =0[and] _[{][s][j][}][m] j_ =1[are][constants.]
comments.explanation: The authors specify m+1 function values {t_{ℓ,0}, ..., t_{ℓ,m}} and m grid points {s_1, ..., s_m}. With m grid points one gets m+1 intervals on the real line, which matches the m+1 values—so this is internally consistent. However, the text does not explicitly say how intervals are formed from the s_j (e.g., whether they are sorted, which value applies to which interval, and whether intervals are open/closed). Later in the experiments the intervals are stated with specific endpoints, but the general construction in Section 2 leaves important boundary conventions implicit. For reproducibility this should be spelled out. The Figure 1 examples (e.g., m=2 with three t-values) help clarify, but not fully.

comments.title: Overclaim: TMAF 'clearly not worse than' traditional DNNs
comments.quote: Since ReLU and Leaky ReLU are included by our DNN as special cases, the proposed DNN is clearly not worse than the traditional ones in practice.
comments.explanation: This claim conflates expressivity of the function class with practical training performance. Containing ReLU as a special case means the hypothesis class is a superset, but training a larger class can easily underperform in practice due to optimization difficulty, overfitting, or poor initialization. Saying it is 'clearly not worse... in practice' overstates what inclusion guarantees. A more accurate statement would be that TMAF has at least the same expressive power.

comments.title: Suspicious identical 1.00 loss for ReLU and Para-ReLU on oscillatory function
comments.explanation: Both ReLU and parametric ReLU report identical final loss of exactly 1.00 on the oscillatory approximation problem. For sin(100πx)+cos(50πx)+sin(πx) on [-1,1], a trivial constant predictor (e.g., zero) would give an MSE near (1/2)(1)+(1/2)(1)+small ≈ 1, so both baselines appear to have converged to essentially predicting the low-frequency component (and perhaps a constant for the rest). This matches Figure 6's narrative but warrants explicit discussion: the exact equality to 1.00 for both suggests rounding, and the reader may suspect the baselines were not trained for long enough or with enough capacity. Given that the stated architecture (3 hidden layers, 400 neurons) is not trivially small, the authors should justify that the baselines have converged, not merely state the result.

comments.title: Tri-diagonal TMAF interval description appears garbled/ambiguous
comments.explanation: The first interval for β_ℓ is stated as (-∞, -2.01 + 99h]. With h=0.02, that gives (-∞, -0.03], but the next set of intervals starts at -2.01 + kh (k=0 gives (-2.01, -1.99]), so the first interval should be (-∞, -2.01] not (-∞, -2.01+99h]. Similarly the third interval (-0.01, ∞) appears to be the right end, so the ordering is inconsistent. This looks like OCR garbling or a transcription error (likely should be (-∞, -2.01] and ((-2.01+99h, -0.01] as the last bounded piece before the (-0.01, ∞) tail, or similar). Either way, the description is not clear enough to reproduce the tri-diagonal TMAF setup.

comments.title: Inclusion of Leaky ReLU as 'special case' not obviously supported by the construction
comments.quote: Since ReLU and Leaky ReLU are included by our DNN as special cases
comments.explanation: For Leaky ReLU σ(t)=t for t>0 and σ(t)=αt for t<0, writing this as D·t with D diagonal requires D to equal 1 for t>0 and α for t<0, which is piecewise constant in t—fine. But the α_ℓ function in equation 6 takes values based on the input of the whole vector η_ℓ; Leaky ReLU applied componentwise corresponds exactly to each diagonal entry depending only on its own scalar input. The Figure 1(a) example with t_{ℓ,0}>0, t_{ℓ,1}=1, m=1, s_1=0 gives σ(t) = t_{ℓ,0}·t for t<0 and σ(t) = 1·t for t>0 — this IS Leaky ReLU (with the roles of negative-side slope playing the role of α). So the claim is fine, but the reader must infer this; it would help to state it explicitly.

comments.title: Recursive definition and notation 'η_L,W,B' used but not fully shown
comments.quote: The ReLU DNN approximation to _f_ at the _L_ -th layer is recursively defined as
comments.explanation: The actual formulas (equation 2, equation 3, equation 4, equation 5, equation 6, equation 7) appear to be missing from the extracted text. This is likely an OCR artifact rather than a paper flaw, but readers relying on this text alone cannot verify the formal specification. The narrative references these equations extensively (e.g., 'equation 5', 'equation 6', 'equation 7') so the mathematical heart of the paper is effectively a black box to a reader with only this text. This is a presentation issue that I flag as a context note, though it is not an issue with the paper itself.

comments.title: Grid points s_j selection strategy based on Gaussian but data is uniform
comments.quote: such that probability over each of the ten intervals is 0.1 with respect to Gaussian distribution. Moreover, we apply BatchNorm1d
comments.explanation: The intervals for α_ℓ are chosen so each has 0.1 probability mass under a standard Gaussian. However the training inputs are uniform on [-2,2]^n. The use of BatchNorm1d may be the reason—after batchnorm, activations are approximately standardized and the Gaussian-based interval design becomes sensible. This is a reasonable design but the logical connection should be stated. As written it could confuse a careful reader who wonders why the authors are matching Gaussian quantiles to uniform data.

comments.title: 'Parametric ReLU' on oscillatory problem: why performs no better than ReLU?
comments.quote: while ReLU and parametric ReLU are not able to approximate the highly oscillating function within reasonable accuracy
comments.explanation: Parametric ReLU has one extra trainable slope per channel for the negative side, which helps with dying ReLU but gives essentially no additional capacity to fit high-frequency oscillations. The paper presents the failure of Para-ReLU as evidence of TMAF's superiority, but the more honest explanation is that the added expressivity of TMAF (piecewise-constant activation with 100 pieces) is simply much greater than adding one parameter per channel. A fair comparison would match parameter counts or expressive capacity. The authors do briefly note 'increasing the number of intervals in TMAF only lead to a few more training parameters,' but do not quantify.

zero_shot__gpt-5.5.label: Zero Shot (gpt-5.5)
zero_shot__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper proposes a simple and potentially useful way to parameterize activation functions by multiplying activations by trainable piecewise-constant coefficients, and the empirical examples suggest that this can improve several of the tested networks. The main concerns are not with the basic construction, which is understandable, but with several claims and definitions being stronger than what is justified. In particular, some mathematical statements need compact-domain or regularity qualifiers, the operator formulation conflicts with the paper’s own use of discontinuous activations, and the training/reproduction details for discontinuous trainable step activations are underspecified. The empirical section also contains a numerical inconsistency in the claimed Gaussian quantile intervals and overstates conclusions such as “not worse” and “robust” based on inclusion of ReLU/Leaky ReLU and limited experiments.

comments.title: Piecewise-constant approximation claim needs a domain/metric qualifier
comments.quote: Since piecewise constant functions can approximate a continuous function within arbitrarily high accuracy, we choose _αℓ_ to be the following step function
comments.explanation: I understand the intended motivation: step functions are a flexible and computationally cheap family for the trainable multiplier _αℓ_, and on bounded activation ranges this is a standard approximation idea. However, the statement as written is too broad. Piecewise-constant functions approximate continuous functions uniformly on compact sets, or in appropriate integral norms under suitable assumptions, but not arbitrary continuous functions on all of R with arbitrarily small uniform error. This matters because _αℓ_ is defined on real-valued preactivations, and later the intervals include unbounded tails. The issue is easily fixed by saying that the approximation is meant on a compact range of preactivations, or in an empirical/Lp sense over the distribution of preactivations.

comments.title: Operator codomain conflicts with discontinuous TMAF activations
comments.quote: First we note that _Dℓ_ could be viewed as a nonlinear operator _Tℓ_ : [ _C_ (R _[d]_ )] _[n][ℓ] →_ [ _C_ (R _[d]_ )] _[n][ℓ]_ , where
comments.explanation: The operator viewpoint is a reasonable way to describe matrix-valued activations. But I checked this against the earlier construction of _αℓ_ as a step function and the later remark that TMAF can include discontinuous functions. If _αℓ_ is piecewise constant with jumps, then for a continuous input function whose values cross a threshold, _αℓ(η)_η_ is generally discontinuous as a function of the original input. Thus _Tℓ_ does not generally map [C(R^d)]^{nℓ} into [C(R^d)]^{nℓ}. The codomain should be weakened, for example to measurable functions, piecewise-continuous functions under additional assumptions, or simply functions R^d→R^{nℓ}. As written, the formal definition contradicts the discontinuous activations that the paper explicitly wants to allow.

comments.title: Training discontinuous step activations is underspecified
comments.quote: In our case, we will choose the grid points _{sj}[m] j_ =1[a priori and train the step values] _[ ∪][L] ℓ_ =1 _[{][t][ℓ,j][}][m] j_ =0 of _αℓ_ .
comments.explanation: The idea of fixing the breakpoints and training only the step values is clear, and the gradients with respect to the selected coefficient _tℓ,j_ are straightforward away from boundaries. What remains unclear is how backpropagation is implemented through the comparisons and discontinuities with respect to the network weights, since the derivative with respect to the preactivation is piecewise constant and undefined at thresholds. A reader can guess a convention, such as ignoring boundary events because they have measure zero or using the automatic differentiation behavior of comparison masks, but this is a key algorithmic choice for a trainable activation method. The paper should specify the derivative/subgradient convention at thresholds, whether gradients are stopped through the interval-selection masks, and how the step values are initialized.

comments.title: Important experimental hyperparameters are not fully specified
comments.quote: In each experiment, we use the same learning rates, stochastic optimization methods, and number NE of epochs (optimization iterations). In particular, the learning rate is 1e-4 from epoch 1 to[NE] 2[and 1e-5 is used from epoch][NE] 2[+ 1][ to][ NE][.][The optimization] method is ADAM (Kingma & Ba (2015)) based on mini-batches of size 500.
comments.explanation: The paragraph does give the optimizer family, batch size, and learning-rate schedule, which is helpful. But the actual value of NE is not specified here, and the paper also does not clearly state Adam hyperparameters, weight initialization, initialization of the trainable activation values _tℓ,j_, or whether the same initialization protocol is used for ReLU, parametric ReLU, and TMAF. These details are especially important because the paper compares optimization behavior and final errors, and because trainable step coefficients can be sensitive to initialization. Without these choices, the numerical results are difficult to reproduce or interpret quantitatively.

comments.title: Claimed equal-probability Gaussian intervals do not match the listed breakpoints
comments.quote: For TMAF _Dℓ_ in equation 5, the function _αℓ_ uses intervals ( _−∞, −_ 1 _._ 4), ( _−_ 1 _._ 4 _, −_ 0 _._ 92], ( _−_ 0 _._ 92 _, −_ 0 _._ 56], ( _−_ 0 _._ 56 _, −_ 0 _._ 26], ( _−_ 0 _._ 26 _,_ 0], (0 _,_ 0 _._ 26], (0 _._ 26 _,_ 0 _._ 56], (0 _._ 56 _,_ 0 _._ 92], (0 _._ 92 _,_ 1 _._ 4], (1 _._ 4 _, ∞_ ) such that probability over each of the ten intervals is 0.1 with respect to Gaussian distribution.
comments.explanation: The use of approximately Gaussian quantile bins is a sensible design choice, especially since BatchNorm is applied. However, for a standard normal distribution the decile cut points are approximately ±1.2816, ±0.8416, ±0.5244, and ±0.2533, not ±1.4, ±0.92, ±0.56, and ±0.26. With the listed values, the two tail intervals have probability about 0.081 each under N(0,1), not 0.1. If the authors intended a nonstandard Gaussian variance or rounded empirical quantiles, that should be stated. As written, the numerical claim about equal 0.1 probability intervals is inconsistent with the listed thresholds.

comments.title: Function-class inclusion does not imply practical performance is never worse
comments.quote: Since ReLU and Leaky ReLU are included by our DNN as special cases, the proposed DNN is clearly not worse than the traditional ones in practice.
comments.explanation: It is correct that, at the level of representable functions, including ReLU and Leaky ReLU as special cases gives TMAF at least the same expressivity under suitable parameter choices. But the conclusion about being “clearly not worse” in practice does not follow. Training is nonconvex, the added activation parameters change the optimization landscape, and finite-time optimization with particular initialization and regularization can perform worse even when the hypothesis class is larger. The claim should be weakened to an expressivity statement, or supported by more systematic empirical evidence showing that optimization and generalization do not degrade.

comments.title: Robustness claim is stronger than the reported evidence
comments.quote: However, our adaptive matrix activation functions are shown to be competitive and more robust in those experiments.
comments.explanation: The experiments do show that TMAF is competitive and sometimes more accurate on the reported runs, which is encouraging. I looked for evidence specifically supporting “more robust,” such as repeated trials with different random seeds, variance/error bars, sensitivity to hyperparameters, perturbation tests, or distribution shifts. The presented results appear to be single-run comparisons of training loss and accuracy, so they support improved performance on those configurations more directly than robustness. The wording should either define and measure robustness, or be softened to say that TMAF performed competitively in the tested examples.
